perturbation.original_text: Notwithstanding the foregoing, either party may terminate this Agreement at any time without liability by providing one hundred eighty (180) days written notice to the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: Notwithstanding the foregoing, either party may terminate this Agreement at any time without liability by providing thirty (30) days written notice to the other party.
perturbation.explanation: The original contract requires 180 days' notice for termination. This change reduces the notification period to 30 days, potentially conflicting with Delaware law regarding reasonable notice for contract termination, especially if such a long-term maintenance agreement is considered requiring a longer notice period to allow the other party to find a replacement service provider.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware Contract Law - Implied Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware law implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract. Reducing the termination notice from 180 days to 30 days may be considered a breach of this covenant if it unreasonably harms the other party, such as preventing them from securing a replacement maintenance provider in a timely manner, rendering the termination clause unenforceable or subject to legal challenge.

General Provisions Part 3 Territorial Applicability and General Rules Г‚В§ 1-301. Territorial applicability; partiesГўВЂВ™ power to choose applicable law. (a) Except as otherwise provided in this section, when a transaction bears a reasonable relation to this State and also to another state or nation the parties may agree that the law either of this State or of such other state or nation shall govern their rights and duties. (b) In the absence of an agreement effective under subsection (a), and except as provided in subsection (c), the Uniform Commercial Code applies to transactions bearing an appropriate relation to this state. (c) If one of the following provisions of the Uniform Commercial Code specifies the applicable law, that provision governs and a contrary agreement is effective only to the extent permitted by the law so specified: (1) Section 2-402; (2) Sections 2A-105 and 2A-106; (3) Section 4-102; (4) Section 4A-507; (5) Section 5-116; (6) Section 8-110; (7) Sections 9-301 through 9-307; (8) Section 12-107. 5A Del. perturbation.justification: The change from 180 days to 30 days notice for contract termination directly contradicts the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing under Delaware law, as it may not provide sufficient time for the other party to secure a replacement service provider, potentially causing unreasonable harm. The law explanation states this reduction 'may be considered a breach of this covenant if it unreasonably harms the other party, such as preventing them from securing a replacement maintenance provider in a timely manner'. Snippet 1 does not directly address the covenant of good faith and fair dealing, but does address that parties may agree to standards.

perturbation.original_text: The Provider shall undertake commercially reasonable efforts to provide the Services and Repairs. The Provider shall provide the Services and Repairs in a professional and workmanlike manner.
perturbation.changed_text: The Provider shall undertake economically efficient efforts to provide the Services and Repairs. The Provider shall provide the Services and Repairs in an adequate manner.
perturbation.explanation: The original language, 'commercially reasonable efforts' and 'professional and workmanlike manner,' establishes a standard of performance. Replacing these terms with 'economically efficient efforts' and 'adequate manner' lowers the bar and introduces ambiguity, potentially contradicting Delaware's implied duty of good faith and fair dealing, especially considering the specialized nature of Fiber Ring Assets maintenance.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware Contract Law - Implied Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware courts interpret the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing to require parties to act reasonably and honestly. Changing the standard from 'commercially reasonable' and 'professional' to 'economically efficient' and 'adequate' suggests a lower standard that may not meet the reasonable expectations of the parties, especially if economically efficient actions undermine the required level of maintenance of Fiber Ring Assets. Thus a court might consider it a violation of the implied covenant.

perturbation.justification: The original text sets a 'commercially reasonable efforts' and 'professional and workmanlike manner' standard. The changed text uses 'economically efficient efforts' and 'adequate manner,' which lowers the standard. This creates a contradiction regarding the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, potentially violating the reasonable expectations of the parties, especially in specialized contexts like Fiber Ring Assets maintenance, as the Law Explanation states. The scraped law snippet 1, Г‚В§ 1-302, states that 'The obligations of good faith, diligence, reasonableness, and care prescribed by the Uniform Commercial Code may not be disclaimed by agreement.' By lowering the standard of care, the changed text arguably attempts to disclaim or diminish these obligations, leading to a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Provider will obtain and maintain appropriate liability insurance in an amount of not less than $1,000,000 combined single limit for accidents or occurrences which cause bodily injury, death or property damage related to the performance of the Services. The insurance policy willname the Company as an additional insured. The Provider will provide the Company with a certificate of insurance issued to evidence such coverage. Such certificate will provide that there shall be no cancellation, non-renewal, or modification of such coverage without thirty days' prior written notice to the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: The Provider will obtain and maintain minimal liability insurance. The Provider will provide the Company with a certificate of insurance issued to evidence such coverage. Such certificate will provide that there shall be no cancellation, non-renewal, or modification of such coverage without ten days' prior written notice to the Company.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause specifies a minimum insurance coverage of $1,000,000 and 30 days' notice for policy changes. The modified clause removes the monetary limit and uses the vague term 'minimal liability insurance' and shortens the notification period to 10 days. This is not in line with business needs for such an operation and conflicts with the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing under Delaware law, and makes the risk for the Company larger than intended.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware Contract Law - Implied Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware law's implied duty of good faith and fair dealing requires parties to act reasonably and honestly. Specifying 'minimal' insurance coverage and a shorter notice period for policy changes may be seen as acting in bad faith, as it significantly increases the risk to the Company without their explicit informed consent, thus possibly breaching the covenant.

perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces a specific insurance amount ($1,000,000) with the vague term 'minimal liability insurance' and reduces the notification period for policy changes from 30 to 10 days. According to the provided Perturbation and Law Explanations, this can be interpreted as acting in bad faith and significantly increases the risk to the Company. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 discusses general contract law and the ability to vary terms, it also states that 'The obligations of good faith, diligence, reasonableness, and care prescribed by the Uniform Commercial Code may not be disclaimed by agreement.' By replacing a definite insurance amount with a vague term, the modification weakens a protection for the Company in a way that could be seen as violating the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing, thus creating a legal contradiction.
